input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a laboratory‑scale gantry crane that mimics harbor cranes from the port of Antwerp. It consists of a small gantry structure, a motorized trolley, a hoist, and a controller that drives the crane. The crane transports small pucks that represent containers from one location to another, providing a low‑cost, reproducible platform for digital‑twin research and education.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin offers CAD models and a kinematic model of the crane and provides services for optimal control of the crane, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation of the physical system. These services support monitoring, control, optimization, and validation activities for both the operators and the physical crane.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is built using freely available, open‑source software. The digital side relies on CAD tools to create the geometry and a kinematic model for motion simulation. Architecture and documentation are expressed with the C4 Model. No proprietary digital‑twin framework is used; the implementation leverages common, off‑the‑shelf tools and libraries to provide the twin services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The gantry‑crane exemplar adopts an ad‑hoc digital‑twin architecture without a dedicated commercial framework. Deployment relies on containerization/virtualisation technologies and the system’s software architecture is described using the C4 model, indicating a loosely coupled arrangement of components rather than a formally orchestrated framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin incorporates geometric CAD models of the gantry crane together with a kinematic analytical model that captures the motion of the crane and its load. The twin provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. All software components are built from freely available, open‑source tools; model relationships are defined between the CAD geometry and the kinematic model to enable simulation‑based control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical side is the lab‑scale gantry crane equipped with motorized actuators that drive the horizontal travel and the hoist mechanism, and a controller that exposes a remote interface for command issuance. The controller allows bidirectional communication with the twin, enabling actuation commands and receiving status feedback. Actuation range covers the full travel of the crane along its rails and the vertical lift range of the hoist, limited by the small‑puck payload.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing subsystem includes a Swing Angle Encoder mounted on the hoist to measure the swinging angle of the load, and an Anemometer attached to the crane structure to measure ambient wind speed. Both sensors provide real‑time measurements that are transmitted via the motor controllers to the Python interface, which forwards the data to the digital twin for logging and validation. Precise sampling rates and accuracy specifications are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by using a kinematic model that is continuously validated against measured motion of the physical crane. The twin includes a continuous validation service that compares sensed state with simulated predictions, allowing detection of modeling errors and fault detection. Validation methods were employed in prior research on continuous validation and fault detection, but specific uncertainty quantification procedures are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Logging Application, a continuously running Python program, reads real‑time state data from the Physical Crane Interface (e.g., position, velocity, load sensor readings) and writes these measurements into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. This flow is initiated by the Physical Crane Interface over a direct Python‑level API and proceeds continuously as the crane operates. No explicit communication protocol beyond the Python interface is documented, and no specific sampling frequency or data‑quality checks are described.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application, a Python program, sends command messages to the Physical Crane Interface, which forwards them to the crane’s low‑level controllers. Commands are generated by higher‑level services such as the Trajectory Generator Service (providing optimal trajectory way‑points) and are transmitted as Python objects over the internal network. Execution feedback is reflected by subsequent state updates logged by the Logging Application, though explicit acknowledgement messages or validation steps are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the digital twin uses an MQTT broker for publish/subscribe signaling between the Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and other components. MQTT (over TCP/IP) provides the messaging backbone. The TimescaleDB database is accessed via its PostgreSQL protocol, and the Grafana dashboard via HTTP. All components reside on a local LAN connecting the Python processes, MQTT broker, database, and dashboard; no cloud‑based networking, bandwidth, latency, or redundancy specifications are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire digital twin is deployed on a single on‑premise personal computer. Docker is used to containerize the TimescaleDB database, the MQTT broker, and the Grafana dashboard. Python processes implement the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, and Continuous Validation services. The deployment relies on the host PC’s compute and storage resources, with container isolation providing basic scalability and fault isolation; no cloud or external edge platforms are used.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The lab‑scale gantry crane digital twin is developed using an ad‑hoc engineering approach rather than a commercial digital‑twin framework. Design choices include extensive use of containerisation and virtualization technologies for easy dissemination of code, and the C4 modelling technique is employed to visualise and describe the system architecture. The exemplar’s source code and documentation are hosted on GitHub, enabling version control, but no specific quality‑assurance, testing, or formal evolution‑management practices are detailed in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)





















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as a 'lab-scale gantry crane' and details its components (gantry structure, motorized trolley, hoist, controller) and its purpose (transporting pucks representing containers). It correctly references the inspiration from 'harbor cranes from the port of Antwerp' and the goal of providing a 'low-cost, reproducible platform'. This is strongly supported by the source text: 'In this exemplar paper we present a lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin' and 'inspired by cranes in the harbor of Antwerp, which move containers'.

The description lists the specific services offered: 'optimal control of the crane, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation'. It correctly identifies the underlying models (CAD, kinematic) and the users (operators, physical system). This is directly supported by the abstract: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation.' It also correctly links these services to monitoring and validation activities.

The extraction correctly states the twin uses 'freely available, open-source software' and specifically mentions CAD tools for geometry, a kinematic model, and the C4 Model for architecture. It also notes the lack of a proprietary framework. This aligns perfectly with the source: 'only relies on other freely available and commonly used software' and 'we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe the system components and services (e.g., 'Logging Application', 'Continuous Validation Service') but do not mention any specific synchronization frequencies, time scales, or latency requirements.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on a single lab-scale gantry crane and its associated digital twin. There is no mention of multiple twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins.

The extraction provides a highly specific description grounded in the text. It correctly identifies the 'ad-hoc approach', the lack of a commercial framework, and the use of containerization and the C4 model. Supported by quotes: 'we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework', 'containerized with Docker', and 'use the C4 Model to visualize and describe the architecture'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source describes a self-contained system running on a personal computer with internal communication protocols (MQTT, SQL). It does not describe 'integration with external systems', 'interoperability standards', or dependencies on external enterprise systems. The system is designed to run locally: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer'.

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the model types (CAD, kinematic) and the specific services provided (optimal control, logging, visualization, validation). It correctly notes the reliance on open-source tools. The mention of 'model relationships' between CAD and kinematic models is a slight inference as the text lists them separately, but the overall description is robust. Supported by quotes: 'The digital side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane, and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation' and 'It is publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software'.

Correctly identifies the motors (cart and hoist), the controller interface, and the bidirectional communication capability. It accurately mentions the payload ('small puck'). However, the statement that the actuation range is 'limited by the small-puck payload' is an unsupported inference; the text implies range is determined by the physical dimensions ('lab-scale'). Supported by quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement' and 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane'.

Correctly lists the Swing Angle Encoder and Anemometer along with their specific measurement functions. Notes the data forwarding to the digital twin and the lack of detailed specs. Supported by quotes: 'Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist', 'Anemometer measures the wind speed'.

Excellent extraction that details the continuous validation process, specifically the comparison of measured versus simulated trajectories and the use of distance metrics/thresholds. It correctly identifies that the system was used in prior research for fault detection and accurately notes that specific uncertainty quantification procedures are absent from the text. Supported by quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold.'

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)

















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
